title: Fix disconnected nodes — audit all 36 pattern diagrams for broken relationship IDs
description: After class name enrichment (LLD-200 through LLD-235), some relationship source/target IDs may point to old class IDs that no longer exist. Audit all 36 pattern definitions in patterns.ts: for each pattern, verify that every relationship.source and relationship.target references a valid class ID in the pattern's classes array. Generate a report of all broken references. Fix each by updating the relationship to point to the correct new class ID. Test by loading each pattern in the UML canvas and confirming all edges render correctly with no orphaned nodes.
acceptanceCriteria: Script or manual audit checks all 36 patterns for relationship ID references
Every relationship.source matches a valid class.id in the same pattern
Every relationship.target matches a valid class.id in the same pattern
No orphaned nodes appear on canvas for any of the 36 patterns
All edges render correctly — no floating endpoints or missing connections
Regression test: load each pattern in dev and verify visual correctness

title: Add edge bundling visual polish — group parallel edges entering same class box
description: When multiple relationships connect to the same class (common in patterns like Abstract Factory with 6+ classes), edges overlap and clutter the diagram. Implement edge bundling: detect parallel edges (same direction, adjacent source/target), group them visually so they enter/exit the class box at slightly offset points rather than overlapping. This improves readability for complex patterns like Abstract Factory (10 classes), Observer (8 classes), and CQRS (10 classes). The implementation should work within the existing SVG canvas renderer in UMLCanvasRenderer.tsx.
acceptanceCriteria: Edges entering the same class box are visually offset (not overlapping)
Edge labels remain readable when bundled
Abstract Factory diagram (10 classes) renders with clear, non-overlapping edges
CQRS diagram (10 classes) renders with clear edge routing
Performance: edge bundling calculation completes in <10ms for 15-class diagrams
No visual regression for simple patterns (Singleton with 1 class)

title: FLIP layout animation — smooth transitions when switching patterns
description: Currently, switching between patterns in the sidebar causes an instant re-render of the UML canvas with no animation. Implement FLIP (First, Last, Invert, Play) animation: when switching patterns, animate classes that exist in both patterns to their new positions, fade out removed classes, fade in new classes. This creates a smooth visual connection between related patterns (e.g., Factory Method -> Abstract Factory). Use requestAnimationFrame for 60fps performance. The animation should be 300-400ms duration with ease-out timing.
acceptanceCriteria: Switching patterns animates class boxes to new positions (300-400ms, ease-out)
Classes present in both patterns animate position smoothly (FLIP technique)
Removed classes fade out, new classes fade in
Relationships animate alongside their connected classes
Animation runs at 60fps (no jank) on mid-range hardware
Animation can be disabled via reduced-motion media query preference

title: Export to PlantUML/Mermaid from UML canvas
description: Users want to copy UML diagrams into their own documentation. Add an export button in the canvas toolbar that generates PlantUML and Mermaid syntax from the current pattern or problem diagram. The export should: (1) Map UML classes to PlantUML class syntax, (2) Map relationships to PlantUML/Mermaid arrows (--|> for inheritance, --o for aggregation, --> for association), (3) Include all attributes and methods with visibility markers, (4) Copy to clipboard with a toast notification. This complements the existing Mermaid DSL Editor (LLD-078) by providing the reverse direction: canvas -> text.
acceptanceCriteria: Export button visible in canvas toolbar with PlantUML and Mermaid options
PlantUML export generates valid @startuml/@enduml syntax for all 36 patterns
Mermaid export generates valid classDiagram syntax for all 36 patterns
All attributes, methods, visibility markers included in export
All relationship types correctly mapped (inheritance, composition, aggregation, association)
Copy-to-clipboard with toast notification confirmation

title: Add pattern comparison mode — side-by-side two patterns
description: LLD interviews often ask candidates to compare patterns (Strategy vs State, Adapter vs Bridge, Decorator vs Proxy). Add a comparison mode: user selects two patterns and sees them side-by-side with their UML diagrams, key differences highlighted, and the confusedWith data rendered as a comparison table. The mode should use a split-pane layout with each pattern's diagram on one side and a differences panel below. This builds on the existing confusedWith field (LLD-106) by providing a visual comparison.
acceptanceCriteria: Comparison mode accessible from pattern browser via 'Compare' button
Split-pane layout shows two pattern UML diagrams side-by-side
Differences panel below shows structured comparison (Intent, Structure, Use When)
confusedWith data rendered as comparison table for pairs that have it
At least 6 common confused pairs work correctly (Strategy/State, Factory/AF, etc.)
Responsive: stacks vertically on screens < 1024px wide

title: Add Mermaid-based diagram editing — edit diagram via DSL, see live UML preview
description: The Mermaid DSL Editor (LLD-078) currently only supports viewing. Extend it to support editing: user can modify the Mermaid classDiagram syntax and see changes reflected live on the UML canvas. This enables rapid prototyping of LLD solutions. Implementation: parse Mermaid classDiagram syntax back into UMLClass[] and UMLRelationship[], then render on the existing canvas. Use the existing MermaidDSLEditor.tsx component as the base. Two-way sync: canvas changes update the DSL, DSL changes update the canvas.
acceptanceCriteria: Mermaid DSL editor panel with syntax highlighting and live preview
Editing Mermaid syntax updates the UML canvas in real-time (<200ms delay)
Canvas interactions (drag, select) update the Mermaid syntax
Parser handles: classes, attributes, methods, relationships (--|>, --o, -->)
Error highlighting: invalid Mermaid syntax shows inline error messages
Reset button restores original pattern Mermaid from pattern data

title: Light mode polish audit — verify all 36 patterns + 33 problems in light mode
description: The LLD module was primarily developed and tested in dark mode. Audit every visual element in light mode for all 36 pattern diagrams and 33 problem diagrams: (1) UML class boxes should have sufficient contrast (dark text on light background), (2) Relationship arrows visible on white canvas, (3) Selection highlight visible, (4) Sidebar active states have sufficient contrast, (5) Code blocks readable with light syntax theme, (6) Properties panel text legible. Fix all contrast issues found. Use WCAG 2.1 AA minimum contrast ratio (4.5:1 for text, 3:1 for large text/graphics).
acceptanceCriteria: All 36 pattern UML diagrams verified visually correct in light mode
All 33 problem diagrams verified visually correct in light mode
UML class boxes have WCAG AA contrast (4.5:1 minimum) in light mode
Relationship arrows visible on white/light canvas background
Code blocks readable with appropriate light syntax theme
No invisible or barely visible UI elements in light mode

title: Performance audit — test with Chess (16 classes), ensure <100ms render
description: The Chess problem is the most complex diagram with 16+ classes (Game, Board, Cell, Piece, King, Queen, Rook, Bishop, Knight, Pawn, Player, Move, Position, GameStatus, MoveValidator, MoveHistory). Profile the UML canvas rendering performance with this diagram: (1) Measure initial render time, (2) Measure re-render on window resize, (3) Measure interaction response (click, drag), (4) Identify bottlenecks in layout calculation, edge routing, or SVG rendering. Target: <100ms initial render, <16ms interaction response (60fps). If needed, implement virtualization for off-screen classes or use canvas instead of SVG for the main rendering path.
acceptanceCriteria: Chess diagram (16 classes) renders in <100ms on mid-range hardware
Interaction response (click, drag) is <16ms (60fps) for Chess diagram
Window resize re-renders in <50ms for Chess diagram
Performance profiling report documenting render pipeline bottlenecks
No jank or dropped frames during pattern switching with 10+ class patterns
Memory usage stable after switching between 10 different patterns

title: Accessibility audit — screen reader support for UML diagrams
description: UML class diagrams are inherently visual. Add screen reader support: (1) Each class box should have aria-label with class name, attributes count, and methods count, (2) Each relationship should have aria-label describing source, target, and relationship type (e.g., 'CreditCardPayment inherits from PaymentStrategy'), (3) Add an accessible text summary of the entire diagram (number of classes, relationships, pattern name), (4) Keyboard navigation: Tab through classes, Enter to show details, Arrow keys for relationship traversal. Follow WAI-ARIA 1.2 graphics role recommendations. Test with VoiceOver on macOS.
acceptanceCriteria: All class boxes have descriptive aria-labels (name, attribute count, method count)
All relationships have aria-labels describing source -> target and type
Diagram summary accessible via aria-label on canvas container
Keyboard navigation: Tab cycles through classes, Enter opens properties
Arrow keys traverse from a class to its connected classes via relationships
VoiceOver on macOS can navigate and describe the full diagram structure

title: Mobile responsiveness — UML canvas on mobile/tablet
description: The UML canvas is currently optimized for desktop (1024px+ width). On mobile/tablet: (1) Classes may overflow the viewport, (2) Touch interactions (pinch-zoom, pan) are not supported, (3) Properties panel overlaps the canvas, (4) Sidebar is too wide. Implement mobile responsive design: collapsible sidebar as drawer, pinch-to-zoom on canvas, swipe to pan, tap to select class, bottom sheet for properties panel. Use CSS media queries and touch event handlers. Target: iPad (768px) and iPhone (375px) breakpoints.
acceptanceCriteria: UML diagrams visible and interactive on iPad (768px) — all 36 patterns work
UML diagrams visible on iPhone (375px) with horizontal scroll for large diagrams
Pinch-to-zoom works on canvas (minimum 0.5x, maximum 3x zoom)
Swipe/drag to pan works on touch devices
Sidebar collapses to drawer/hamburger menu on screens < 768px
Properties panel renders as bottom sheet on mobile, not overlapping canvas
